17 MAIN STREET | "Main Street Sag Harbor" | INSPIRED BY KRS ONE

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1]

[1] "Sag Harbor, built in 1930, let me set the stage" → Directly from: "Was built in 1930." Unknown Author. "CRIS."

[6] "Main Street to the wharf was a dirt road back in the day" → Directly from: "Main Street to the wharf was a dirt road in 1898." Roth. (2018) Images of America Sag Harbor, p. 21.

[2] "Daniel Fordham's old inn, origin long anterior to the Revolution" → Directly from: "Of origin long anterior to the Revolution, its history is coeval with the early days of Sag-Harbor." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 39.

[2] "Aunt Dence remembered dancing there as a girl, that's the contribution" → Drawn from: "Aunt Dence Fordham, grandmother of George Bassett, deceased, remembered .to have danced there when a girl." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 39.

[3] "Peletiah Duke Fordham owned another early hotel on the corner scene" → Directly from: "Peletiah (Duke) Fordham owned another early hotel which stood on the corner of Main and West Water Streets at the foot of the village." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 198.

[3] "Main and West Water Streets, a roomy and convenient house, rooms and a bar well-supplied, you know what I mean" → Directly from: "Duke Fordham's hotel was built before the American Revolution and known as 'a roomy and convenient house that contained numerous rooms for lodging plus a bar that was well supplied to accommodate visitors.'" Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 198.

[Chorus]

[7] "Flames ascending up to heaven in one vast blazing pyramid of light" → Directly from: "the flames in their unrestrained and unconquerable fury bursting forth on every side and ascending up to heaven in one vast blazing pyramid of light." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 109.

[7] "Volumes of black dense smoke shrouded the skies, hiding the stars from sight" → Directly from: "while volumes of black dense smoke shrouded the skies in gloom, hiding the stars from sight." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 109.

[7] "Phoenix-like, rose again from the ashes — Sag Harbor stand tall" → Directly from: "Sag Harbor, phoenix-like, rose again from the ashes." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 109.

[6] "From the wharf to Main Street, we remember it all" → Drawn from: "Main Street to the wharf was a dirt road in 1898." Roth. (2018) Images of America Sag Harbor, p. 21.

[Verse 2]

[8] "Oakley family built the Nassau Hotel, eighteen forty-seven on the block" → Directly from: "The Oakley family's 1847 Nassau Hotel was destroyed by fire in February 1877." Roth. (2018) Images of America Sag Harbor, p. 33.

[8] "February eighteen seventy-seven, fire came and wouldn't stop" → Directly from: "The Oakley family's 1847 Nassau Hotel was destroyed by fire in February 1877." Roth. (2018) Images of America Sag Harbor, p. 33.

[3] "Betsey Jose's Fort on Meadow Street, she flew a flag of defiance from the roof" → Directly from: "Betsey barricaded the house and flew a flag of defiance from the roof and didn't surrender until terms were agreed on." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 198.

[3] "Barricaded the house against the railroad — seventeen years the proof" → Drawn from: "Although Betsey retreated, she won the fight. For 17 years the house stood, a defiant fortress." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 198.

[3] "Temperance movement took the village, Duke Fordham closed the bar" → Drawn from: "During those years a great temperance movement took place in Sag Harbor, and along with many business men in the village, Duke Fordham was, apparently, deeply involved." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 198.

[3] "No intoxicating liquor of any kind — refectory with relishes, raising the bar" → Directly from: "No intoxicating liquor of any kind would be sold in his establishment. The bar was fitted up as a refectory, where refreshments and relishes of all kinds were constantly on hand." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 198.

[Bridge]

[6] "Octagonal bandstand at the foot of the wharf, moved to the park with pride" → Directly from: "In 1908, the octagonal bandstand at the foot of the wharf was moved to the park with donations from the Ladies' Village Improvement Society (LVIS) and Mary Gertrude Edson Aldrich and weekly concerts continued." Roth. (2018) Images of America Sag Harbor, p. 21.

[10] "Vote no licenses and every place selling liquor will be closed, Kilgore cried" → Directly from: "His conviction: 'vote no licenses and every place selling liquor will be closed.'" Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 268.

[10] "Dives and speakeasies sprung up, kitchen cabinets in every home" → Directly from: "Dives and speakeasies sprung up" and "in many houses 'kitchen cabinets' were established." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 268.

[10] "One minute after midnight, fourteen freshly painted bars — no longer alone" → Directly from: "One minute after midnight on October first, 14 sets of lights were turned on, revealing as many freshly painted bars and shiny sets of glassware, all ready for business." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 268.

[Outro]

[2] "From Daniel Fordham's wheels that broke on the road to Sagaponack" → Drawn from: "The old house and inn of Daniel Fordham was sold to some one who tradition tells started to move. it to Sagg. When it got as far as the lot south of E. C. Rogers, deceased, the wheels broke, and the mover declared he would go no farther with it." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 39.

[7] "To the phoenix rising twice — Sag Harbor always comes back" → Drawn from: "Sag Harbor, phoenix-like, rose again from the ashes." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 109.
